sourceQuestion:
Consider the following relation Cinema(theater, address, capacity).

Which of the following options will be needed at the end of the SQL query

SELECT P1.address
FROM Cinema P1

such that it always finds the addresses of theaters with maximum capacity?

A. WHERE P1.capacity >= All (select P2.capacity from Cinema P2)
B. WHERE P1.capacity >= Any (select P2.capacity from Cinema P2)
C. WHERE P1.capacity >= All (select max(P2.capacity) from Cinema P2)
D. WHERE P1.capacity >= Any (select max(P2.capacity) from Cinema P2)
sourceAnswer: A. WHERE P1.capacity >= All (select P2.capacity from Cinema P2)

parsed.stem:
Consider the following relation Cinema(theater, address, capacity).

Which of the following options will be needed at the end of the SQL query

SELECT P1.address
FROM Cinema P1

such that it always finds the addresses of theaters with maximum capacity?

options.text: WHERE P1.capacity >= All (select P2.capacity from Cinema P2)
WHERE P1.capacity >= Any (select P2.capacity from Cinema P2)
WHERE P1.capacity >= All (select max(P2.capacity) from Cinema P2)
WHERE P1.capacity >= Any (select max(P2.capacity) from Cinema P2)

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following database table named top_scorer:

player       | country   | goals
-------------|-----------|------
Klose        | Germany   | 16
Ronaldo      | Brazil    | 15
G.Muller     | Germany   | 14
Fontaine     | France    | 13
Pele         | Brazil    | 12
Klinsman     | Germany   | 11
Koosis       | Hungary   | 10
Batistuta    | Argentina | 10
Cubillas     | Peru      | 10
Lato         | Poland    | 10
Lineker      | England   | 10
T.Muller     | Germany   | 10
Ralas        | Germany   | 10

Consider the following SQL query:
SELECT ta.player FROM top_scorer AS ta
WHERE ta.goals > ALL (SELECT tb.goals FROM top_scorer AS tb WHERE tb.country = 'Spain')
AND ta.goals > ANY (SELECT tc.goals FROM top_scorer AS tc WHERE tc.country = 'Germany')

The number of tuples returned by the above SQL query is ______.

A. 6
B. 5
C. 3
D. 1

The number of tuples returned by the above SQL query is ______.

sourceQuestion:
Consider a relational database containing the following schemas. The primary key of each table is indicated by underlining the constituent fields.

Catalogue(sno, pno, cost)
Suppliers(sno, sname, location)
Parts(pno, pname, part_spec)

Suppliers table (sample):
sno | sname                    | location
S1  | M/s Royal furniture      | Delhi
S2  | M/s Balaji furniture     | Bangalore
S3  | M/s Premium furniture    | Chennai

Parts table (sample):
pno | pname   | part_spec
P1  | Table   | Wood
P2  | Chair   | Wood
P3  | Table   | Steel
P4  | Almirah | Steel
P5  | Almirah | Wood

Catalogue table (sample):
sno | pno | cost
S1  | P1  | 150
S1  | P2  | 100
S2  | P4  | 200
S2  | P1  | 150
S3  | P2  | 150
S3  | P3  | 300
S3  | P4  | 250

The primary key of each table is indicated by underlining the constituent fields.

SELECT s.sno, s.sname
FROM Suppliers s, Catalogue c
WHERE s.sno = c.sno AND cost > (SELECT AVG(cost)
                                 FROM Catalogue
                                 WHERE sno = s.sno)
GROUP BY s.sno, s.sname;

The number of rows returned by the above SQL query is

A. 4
B. 5
C. 2
D. 3

The number of rows returned by the above SQL query is

sourceQuestion:
The relation scheme given below is used to store information about the employees of a company, where empId is the key and deptId indicates the department to which the employee is assigned. Each employee is assigned to exactly one department.

emp(empId, name, gender, salary, deptId)

Consider the following SQL query:

select deptId, count(*)
from emp
where gender = 'female' AND salary > (select avg(salary) from emp)
group by deptId

The above query gives, for each department, the number of:
A. employees in the department
B. employees in the company
C. female employees in the department whose salary is greater than the average salary of all employees in the company
D. female employees in the company whose salary is greater than the average salary of all employees in the company
sourceAnswer: C. female employees in the department whose salary is greater than the average salary of all employees in the company

parsed.stem:

emp(empId, name, gender, salary, deptId)

Consider the following SQL query:

select deptId, count(*)
from emp
where gender = 'female' AND salary > (select avg(salary) from emp)
group by deptId

The above query gives, for each department, the number of:

options.text: female employees in the department whose salary is greater than the average salary of all employees in the company
female employees in the company whose salary is greater than the average salary of all employees in the company

sourceQuestion:
Consider the relational database with the following four schemas and their respective instances:

Student(sNo, sName, dNo)
Dept(dNo, dName)
Course(cNo, cName, dNo)
Register(sNo, cNo)

Student:
sNo | sName   | dNo
S01 | James   | D01
S02 | Rocky   | D01
S03 | Jackson | D02
S04 | June    | D01
S05 | Milli   | D02

Dept:
dNo | dName
D01 | CSE
D02 | EEE

Course:
cNo | cName | dNo
C11 | OS    | D01
C12 | OS    | D01
C21 | DE    | D02
C22 | PT    | D02
C23 | CV    | D03

Register:
sNo | cNo
S01 | C12
S02 | C11
S03 | C21
S03 | C22
S04 | C11
S04 | C12
S05 | C21

Consider the following SQL query:

SELECT sNo
FROM Student AS S
WHERE dNo = 'D01'
  AND sNo IN (
    SELECT sNo FROM Register AS R
    WHERE cNo IN (
      SELECT cNo FROM Course WHERE dNo = 'D01'
    )
  )
EXCEPT
SELECT sNo
FROM Student
WHERE dNo = 'D01'
  AND sNo NOT IN (
    SELECT sNo FROM Register
    WHERE cNo IN (
      SELECT cNo FROM Course WHERE dNo = 'D01'
    )
  )

The number of rows returned by the above SQL query is ____________.
A. 4
B. 5
C. 0
D. 3

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following relational schema:
Students (stNo: integer, name: string, age: integer, cgpa: real)
Courses (cname: integer, credits: integer)
Enrolled (stNo: integer, cname: integer, grade: string)

Which of the following options is/are correct SQL query/queries to retrieve the names of the students enrolled in course number (i.e., cname) 1470?

A. SELECT S.name
   FROM Students S
   WHERE S.stNo IN (SELECT E.stNo
                    FROM Enrolled E
                    WHERE E.cname = 1470
                    AND E.stNo = S.stNo)

B. SELECT S.name
   FROM Students S
   WHERE EXISTS (SELECT *
                 FROM Enrolled E
                 WHERE E.cname = 1470
                 AND E.stNo = S.stNo)

C. SELECT S.name
   FROM Students S
   WHERE S.stNo IN (SELECT COUNT(*)
                    FROM Students
                    WHERE E.cname = 1470
                    AND E.stNo = S.stNo)

D. SELECT S.name
   FROM Students S NATURAL JOIN Enrolled E
   WHERE E.cname = 1470
sourceAnswer: B. SELECT S.name FROM Students S WHERE EXISTS (SELECT * FROM Enrolled E WHERE E.cname = 1470 AND E.stNo = S.stNo)

parsed.stem:
Consider the following relational schema:
Students (stNo: integer, name: string, age: integer, cgpa: real)
Courses (cname: integer, credits: integer)
Enrolled (stNo: integer, cname: integer, grade: string)

Which of the following options is/are correct SQL query/queries to retrieve the names of the students enrolled in course number (i.e., cname) 1470?

options.text: SELECT S.name FROM Students S WHERE S.stNo IN (SELECT E.stNo FROM Enrolled E WHERE E.cname = 1470 AND E.stNo = S.stNo)
SELECT S.name FROM Students S WHERE EXISTS (SELECT * FROM Enrolled E WHERE E.cname = 1470 AND E.stNo = S.stNo)
SELECT S.name FROM Students S WHERE S.stNo IN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Students WHERE E.cname = 1470 AND E.stNo = S.stNo)
SELECT S.name FROM Students S NATURAL JOIN Enrolled E WHERE E.cname = 1470

sourceQuestion:
An OTT company is maintaining a large disk-based relational database of different movies with the following schema:

Movie (ID, CustomerRating)
Genre (ID, Name)
Movie_Genre (MovieID, GenreID)

Consider the following SQL query on the relation database above:

SELECT *
FROM Movie, Genre, Movie_Genre
WHERE Movie.CustomerRating > 4 AND
      Movie_Genre.MovieID = Movie.ID AND
      Movie_Genre.GenreID = Genre.ID;

The SQL query can be sped up using which of the following indexing options?

A. B+ tree on all the attributes.
B. Hash index on Genre.Name and B+ tree on the remaining attributes.
C. Hash index on Movie.CustomerRating and B+ tree on the remaining attributes.
D. Hash index on all the attributes.
sourceAnswer: B. Hash index on Genre.Name and B+ tree on the remaining attributes.

parsed.stem:
An OTT company is maintaining a large disk-based relational database of different movies with the following schema:

Movie (ID, CustomerRating)
Genre (ID, Name)
Movie_Genre (MovieID, GenreID)

Consider the following SQL query on the relation database above:

SELECT *
FROM Movie, Genre, Movie_Genre
WHERE Movie.CustomerRating > 4 AND
      Movie_Genre.MovieID = Movie.ID AND
      Movie_Genre.GenreID = Genre.ID;

The SQL query can be sped up using which of the following indexing options?

options.text: B+ tree on all the attributes.
Hash index on Genre.Name and B+ tree on the remaining attributes.
Hash index on Movie.CustomerRating and B+ tree on the remaining attributes.
Hash index on all the attributes.

sourceQuestion:
A table T1 in a relational database has the following rows and columns:

Roll no. | Marks
---------|------
    1    |  10
    2    |  20
    3    |  30
    4    | NULL

The following sequence of SQL statements was successfully executed on table T1:

Update T1 set marks = marks + 5;
Select avg(marks) from T1;

What is the output of the select statement?

A. 18.75
B. 20
C. 25
D. Null

sourceQuestion:
Consider two tables in a relational database with columns and rows as follows:

Student Table:
Roll_no | Name | Dept_id
1       | ABC  | 1
2       | DEF  | 1
3       | GHI  | 2
4       | JKL  | 3

Department Table:
Dept_id | Dept_name
1       | A
2       | B
3       | C

Roll_no is the primary key of the Student table, Dept_id is the primary key of the Department table and Student.Dept_id is a foreign key referencing Department.Dept_id.

What will happen if we try to execute the following two SQL statements?

1. update Student set Dept_id = Null where Roll_no = 1
2. update Student set Dept_id = 5 where Roll_no = 4

A. Both i and ii will fail
B. i will fail but ii will succeed
C. i will succeed but ii will fail
D. Both i and ii will succeed
sourceAnswer: C. i will succeed but ii will fail

sourceQuestion:
In an inventory management system implemented at a trading corporation, there are several tables designed to hold all the information. Amongst these, the following two tables hold information on which items are supplied by which suppliers, and which warehouse keeps which items along with the stock-level of these items.

Supply = (supplierid, itemcode)
Inventory = (itemcode, warehouse, stocklevel)

For the purposes required by the management, the following SQL query has been written:

Select distinct STMP.supplierid
From Supply as STMP
Where not exists
  (Select * from Inventory as INVY
   Where STMP.itemcode = INVY.itemcode
   And STMP.itemcode = 'Nagpur'
   And Inventory.itemcode = 'Nagpur')

For the warehouse at Nagpur, this query will find all suppliers who:

A. do not supply any item
B. supply exactly one item
C. supply one or more items
D. supply two or more items
sourceAnswer: C. supply one or more items

sourceQuestion:
Consider a database with three relation instances shown below. The primary keys for the Drivers and Cars relation are did and cid respectively and the records are stored in ascending order of these primary keys as given in the tables. No indexing is available in the database.

D: Drivers relation
did | dname         | rating | age
22  | Karthikeyan   | 7      | 25
29  | Salman        | 1      | 33
31  | Boris         | 8      | 55
32  | Amuldt        | 8      | 25
58  | Schumacher    | 10     | 35
64  | Sachin        | 7      | 35
71  | Senna         | 10     | 16
74  | Sachin        | 9      | 35
85  | Rahul         | 3      | 25
95  | Ralph         | 3      | 53

B: Reserves relation
did | Cid | day
22  | 101 | 10/10/06
22  | 102 | 10/10/06
22  | 103 | 08/10/06
22  | 104 | 07/10/06
31  | 102 | 10/11/06
31  | 103 | 06/11/06
31  | 104 | 12/11/06
64  | 101 | 05/09/06
64  | 102 | 08/09/06
74  | 103 | 08/09/06

C: Cars relation
Cid | Cname   | colour
101 | Renault | blue
102 | Renault | red
103 | Renault | green
104 | Jaguar  | red

What is the output of the following SQL query?

select D.dname
from Drivers D
where D.did = (select R.did
               from Reserves R, Cars C
               where R.Cid = C.cid
               and C.colour = 'green')

A. Karthikeyan, Boris
B. Sachin, Salmon
C. Karthikeyan, Boris, Sachin
D. Schumacher, Senna

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following relational schema:
- School(school-id, sch-name, sch-address, sch-phone)
- Enrolment(school-id, sch-name, sch-roll-no, erollno, examname)
- ExamResult(sch-id, sch-roll-no, erollno, examname, marks)

What does the following SQL query output?

SELECT sch-name, COUNT(*)
FROM School C, Enrolment E, ExamResult R
WHERE E.school-id = C.school-id
  AND E.examname = R.examname
  AND E.erollno = R.erollno
  AND R.marks = 100
  AND E.school-id IN (SELECT school-id
                      FROM Enrolment
                      GROUP BY school-id
                      HAVING COUNT(*) > 100)
GROUP BY sch-name;

A. for each school with more than 200 students appearing in exams, the name of the school and the number of 100s scored by its students
B. for each school with more than 200 students in it, the name of the school and the number of 100s scored by its students
C. for each school with more than 200 students in it, the name of the school and the number of its students scoring 100 in at least one exam
D. nothing; the query has a syntax error
sourceAnswer: A. for each school with more than 200 students appearing in exams, the name of the school and the number of 100s scored by its students

parsed.stem:
Consider the following relational schema:
- School(school-id, sch-name, sch-address, sch-phone)
- Enrolment(school-id, sch-name, sch-roll-no, erollno, examname)
- ExamResult(sch-id, sch-roll-no, erollno, examname, marks)

What does the following SQL query output?

SELECT sch-name, COUNT(*)
FROM School C, Enrolment E, ExamResult R
WHERE E.school-id = C.school-id
  AND E.examname = R.examname
  AND E.erollno = R.erollno
  AND R.marks = 100
  AND E.school-id IN (SELECT school-id
                      FROM Enrolment
                      GROUP BY school-id
                      HAVING COUNT(*) > 100)
GROUP BY sch-name;

options.text: for each school with more than 200 students appearing in exams, the name of the school and the number of 100s scored by its students
for each school with more than 200 students in it, the name of the school and the number of 100s scored by its students
for each school with more than 200 students in it, the name of the school and the number of its students scoring 100 in at least one exam
nothing; the query has a syntax error

sourceQuestion:
For the schedule given below, which of the following is correct?

Step | Operation
1    | Read A  (T1)
2    | Read B  (T2)
3    | Write A (T1)
4    | Read A  (T2)
5    | Write A (T2)
6    | Write B (T1)
7    | Read B  (T1)
8    | Write B (T2)

A. This schedule is serializable and can occur in a scheme using 2PL protocol
B. This schedule is serializable but cannot occur in a scheme using 2PL protocol
C. This schedule is not serializable but can occur in a scheme using 2PL protocol
D. This schedule is not serializable and cannot occur in a scheme using 2PL protocol
sourceAnswer: B. This schedule is serializable but cannot occur in a scheme using 2PL protocol

parsed.stem:
For the schedule given below, which of the following is correct?

Step | Operation
1    | Read A  (T1)
2    | Read B  (T2)
3    | Write A (T1)
4    | Read A  (T2)
5    | Write A (T2)
6    | Write B (T1)
7    | Read B  (T1)
8    | Write B (T2)

options.text: This schedule is serializable and can occur in a scheme using 2PL protocol
This schedule is serializable but cannot occur in a scheme using 2PL protocol
This schedule is not serializable but can occur in a scheme using 2PL protocol
This schedule is not serializable and cannot occur in a scheme using 2PL protocol

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following scenarios may lead to an irrecoverable error in a database system?

A. A transaction writes a data item after it is read by an uncommitted transaction
B. A transaction reads a data item after it is read by an uncommitted transaction
C. A transaction reads a data item after it is written by an uncommitted transaction
D. A transaction writes a data item after it is written by an uncommitted transaction
sourceAnswer: C. A transaction reads a data item after it is written by an uncommitted transaction

parsed.stem: Which of the following scenarios may lead to an irrecoverable error in a database system?

options.text: A transaction writes a data item after it is read by an uncommitted transaction
A transaction reads a data item after it is read by an uncommitted transaction
A transaction reads a data item after it is written by an uncommitted transaction
A transaction writes a data item after it is written by an uncommitted transaction

sourceQuestion:
Consider three data items D1, D2, and D3, and the following execution schedule of transactions T1, T2, and T3. R(D) and W(D) denote the actions reading and writing the data item D respectively.

T1          | T2          | T3
R(D1);      |             |
W(D1);      |             |
            | R(D2);      |
            | W(D2);      |
            |             | R(D3);
            |             | W(D3);
R(D2);      |             |
W(D2);      |             |
            | R(D3);      |
            | W(D3);      |
            |             | R(D1);
            |             | W(D1);

Which of the following statements is correct?

A. The schedule is serializable as T2; T3; T1
B. The schedule is serializable as T1; T2; T3
C. The schedule is serializable as T3; T2; T1
D. The schedule is not serializable
sourceAnswer: D. The schedule is not serializable

parsed.stem:
Consider three data items D1, D2, and D3, and the following execution schedule of transactions T1, T2, and T3. R(D) and W(D) denote the actions reading and writing the data item D respectively.

T1          | T2          | T3
R(D1);      |             |
W(D1);      |             |
            | R(D2);      |
            | W(D2);      |
            |             | R(D3);
            |             | W(D3);
R(D2);      |             |
W(D2);      |             |
            | R(D3);      |
            | W(D3);      |
            |             | R(D1);
            |             | W(D1);

Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: The schedule is serializable as T2; T3; T1
The schedule is serializable as T1; T2; T3
The schedule is serializable as T3; T2; T1

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following log sequence of two transactions on a bank account, with initial balance 13000, that transfer 3000 to a mortgage payment and then apply a 5% interest.
1. T1 start
2. T1 B old = 13000 new = 10000
3. T1 M old = 0 new = 2000
4. T1 commit
5. T2 start
6. T2 B old = 10000 new = 10500
7. T2 commit

Suppose the database system crashes just before log record 7 is written. When the system is restarted, which one statement is true of the recovery procedure?

A. We must redo log record B to set B to 10000
B. We must undo log record B to set B to 10000 and then redo log records 2 and 3
C. We need not redo log records 2 and 3 because transaction T1 has committed
D. We can apply redo and undo operations in any order because they are idempotent
sourceAnswer: C. We need not redo log records 2 and 3 because transaction T1 has committed

parsed.stem:
Consider the following log sequence of two transactions on a bank account, with initial balance 13000, that transfer 3000 to a mortgage payment and then apply a 5% interest.
1. T1 start
2. T1 B old = 13000 new = 10000
3. T1 M old = 0 new = 2000
4. T1 commit
5. T2 start
6. T2 B old = 10000 new = 10500
7. T2 commit

Suppose the database system crashes just before log record 7 is written. When the system is restarted, which one statement is true of the recovery procedure?

options.text: We must redo log record B to set B to 10000
We must undo log record B to set B to 10000 and then redo log records 2 and 3
We need not redo log records 2 and 3 because transaction T1 has committed
We can apply redo and undo operations in any order because they are idempotent

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following schedules involving two transactions. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

S1: r1(X) r1(Y) r2(Y) r2(X) r3(X) r3(Y) w1(X) w2(Y) w3(Y) w1(Y)
S2: r1(X) r2(X) r1(Y) r2(Y) w1(X) w2(Y) w1(Y) w2(X)
S3: r1(X) r2(X) r1(Y) r2(Y) w2(X) w1(Y) w2(Y) w1(X)
S4: r1(X) r2(X) r1(Y) r2(Y) w1(X) w1(Y) w2(X) w2(Y)

A. S1 and S2
B. S2 and S3
C. S3 only
D. S4 only

S1: r1(X) r1(Y) r2(Y) r2(X) r3(X) r3(Y) w1(X) w2(Y) w3(Y) w1(Y)
S2: r1(X) r2(X) r1(Y) r2(Y) w1(X) w2(Y) w1(Y) w2(X)
S3: r1(X) r2(X) r1(Y) r2(Y) w2(X) w1(Y) w2(Y) w1(X)
S4: r1(X) r2(X) r1(Y) r2(Y) w1(X) w1(Y) w2(X) w2(Y)

sourceQuestion:
Consider two transactions T1 and T2, and four schedules S1, S2, S3, S4 of T1 and T2 as given below:

T1: r1(X) r1(Y) r1(X) w1(Y) w1(X)
T2: r2(Y) w2(Y) w2(X)

S1: r1(X) r1(Y) r2(Y) w2(Y) r1(X) w1(Y) w2(X) w1(X)
S2: r1(X) r1(Y) r2(Y) r1(X) w1(Y) w1(X) w2(Y) w2(X)
S3: r1(X) r2(Y) r1(Y) w2(Y) r1(X) w2(X) w1(Y) w1(X)
S4: r2(Y) r1(X) r1(Y) r1(X) w2(Y) w1(Y) w2(X) w1(X)

Which of the above schedules are conflict-serializable?

A. S1 and S2
B. S2 and S3
C. S3 only
D. S4 only

parsed.stem:

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following concurrency control protocols ensure both conflict serializability and freedom from deadlock?

I. 2-phase locking
II. Time-stamp ordering

A. I only
B. II only
C. Both I and II
D. Neither I nor II

I. 2-phase locking

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following schedule for transactions T1, T2 and T3:

T1          T2          T3
Read(X)
            Read(Y)
                        Read(Y)
            Write(Y)
                        Write(X)
Read(X)
Write(X)

Any non-serial interleaving of T1 and T2 for concurrent execution leads to

A. a non-serializable schedule
B. a schedule that is not conflict serializable
C. a conflict serializable schedule
D. a schedule for which a precedence graph cannot be drawn

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following transaction involving two bank accounts x and y:
  read(x); x := x - 50; write(x); read(y); y := y + 50; write(y)

The constraint that the sum of the accounts x and y should remain constant is that of
A. Atomicity
B. Consistency
C. Isolation
D. Durability

sourceQuestion:
Consider a simple checkpointing protocol and the following set of operations in the log:
(start, T4); (write, T4, y, 2, 3); (start, T1); (commit, T4); (write, T1, z, 5, 7); (checkpoint); (start, T2); (write, T2, x, 1, 9); (commit, T2); (start, T3); (write, T3, z, 7, 2);

If a crash happens now and the system tries to recover using undo and redo operations, what are the contents of the undo list and redo list?
A. Undo: T3, T1; Redo: T2, T4
B. Undo: T3, T1; Redo: T2
C. Undo: T3; Redo: T2, T1
D. Undo: T3; Redo: T2
sourceAnswer: C. Undo: T3; Redo: T2, T1

options.text: Undo: T3, T1; Redo: T2, T4

sourceQuestion:
Consider the partial Schedule S involving two transactions T1 and T2. Only the read and the write operations have been shown. The read operation on data item P is denoted by read(P) and the write operation on data item P is denoted by write(P).

Schedule S:
Time Instance | T1        | T2
1             | read(A)   |
2             | write(A)  |
3             |           | read(C)
4             |           | write(C)
5             | read(B)   |
6             | write(B)  |
7             |           | read(A)
8             |           | write(A)
9             | read(B)   |

Suppose that T1 fails immediately after time instance 9. Which of the following statements is correct?
A. T2 must be aborted and then T1 and T2 must be restarted to ensure transaction atomicity
B. Schedule S is recoverable and cannot ensure transaction atomicity
C. Only T2 must be aborted and then restarted to ensure transaction atomicity
D. Schedule S is recoverable and can ensure transaction atomicity and nothing else needs to be done
sourceAnswer: A. T2 must be aborted and then T1 and T2 must be restarted to ensure transaction atomicity

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following is NOT a part of the ACID properties of database transactions?
A. Atomicity
B. Consistency
C. Isolation
D. Deadlock-freedom

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two-phase locking protocol. Suppose a transaction T accesses (for read or write operations) a certain set of objects {O_1, O_2, ..., O_k}. This is done in the following manner:
- Step 1: T acquires exclusive locks to O_1, O_2, ..., O_k in increasing order of their addresses.
- Step 2: The required operations are performed.
- Step 3: All locks are released.

This protocol will:
A. guarantee serializability and deadlock-freedom
B. guarantee neither serializability nor deadlock-freedom
C. guarantee serializability but not deadlock-freedom
D. guarantee deadlock-freedom but not serializability

- Step 2: The required operations are performed.
- Step 3: All locks are released.

sourceQuestion:
Suppose a database schedule S involves transactions T_1, T_2, ..., T_n. Construct the precedence graph of S. If S is serializable, which of the following is guaranteed to yield a serial schedule?
A. Topological order
B. Breadth-first order
C. Depth-first order
D. Ascending order of the transaction indices

options.text: Ascending order of the transaction indices
